item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is an old page and the author probably won't see this, but perfect. I had actually found the SCP article itself rather lacklustre compared to some of the other ones I had been reading, but this tale gives it the perfect lift.

Anyone think the Foundation will try and figure out a way to use the sack and capture the Third Brother? Maybe the Old Man hasn't told them about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm… Anyone think the third brother is hanging out in the cave to SCP-2935?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My own interpretation would be that his current old appearance is how he would have looked at the time of his natural death- and that he is thus frozen at that point.

EDIT: Woop, massive necro, sorry about that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this, +1

However, I'm left to wonder why the man appears as an octogenarian if he won the card games as a young man. Is it because he only ran out of water/cards/bag in his eighties, and was only cursed with eternal life once he could no longer keep the Brothers at bay? Even then, wouldn't he keep aging, since all the Brothers are really doing is not taking him to the afterlife, they never actually made him immortal? Shouldn't he just keep growing older and older-looking, to an unnatural degree, but never die and still be able to walk?

Just a minor nit-pick, but the tale (or the original SCP) might benefit from an explanation.

item.title: @ Thank you so much Dmatix for writing this tale
item.content:encoded: This was good, very very good. I'm surprised I hadn't noticed this before (Thanks Zyn for pointing this out to me). This was an amazing read, it filled up the information 1440 didn't give and it did a mighty good job doing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was fantastic. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent tale

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, and its source are two of my all-time faves. After listening to the latest KaktusKast, I came back to revisit. Still as compelling as the first time. This has such an impact for me. Possibly because I would've made the challenge. I might have gotten greedy too. Such is man.

I upvoted this the first, second, and most recent visit. Alas it only lets me once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is not a bad twist. But this tale stands out by the way is told and not by the shock effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is what I was thinking. Put yourself in the sack old man!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if the old man is the All-Death since he causes the destruction of everything around him, and he simply doesn't realize it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice job. Also, "It had once last use," I'm guessing, is supposed to be one last use.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know, the note in the beginning shouldn't be there IMHO. It would be better in the comments, secondo me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Crosstests .-.

But you can write a tale for it if you want. Heck, most tales come from "why doesn't" and "what if" questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why doesnt the foundation just use SCP 738 the guy who makes deals to put this guy out of his misery?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic. Can't believe I hadn't read it yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And you made an "it's" error. For shame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By the way, I know this is just a minor error, and necromancy and all that, but "who's origin was not of this world" should be "whose". I just thought that it might be helpful to say so. I really liked the folkloric theme and it's structure, I really enjoyed the way you put it together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a silly question in the least. The old man, even in his current state, is still a man driven by fear- he's too afraid to use the sack's final use on anything other than the third brother because if it doesn't work, he's just lost his last chance. It might very well be that the sack could have been used in a better manner, but the old man won't because he's still too afraid to do so. Besides, the things trapped in the sack might still be inside, and they won't be at all happy to see him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, and original SCP are both very good. It's cool to have something that fits the old folklore feel and still fits the SCP universe as well.

Silly question of the day: What if, instead of capturing the third in his sack, he were to use the final use to capture himself? On the one hand, it's a silly idea, on the other hand maybe it's what the brothers want from him, as it's a sort of humble resignation, a recognition that he can never beat them again, will never lure them in, and can only save humanity by meekly succumbing to them the way he didn't the first time. Unlike 'catching death in a sack', which even if it's for his own destruction is still a grandiose path, it would be a real admission of defeat and surrender.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow.

Full disclosure, I downvoted for the first four paragraphs (because I thought I knew where you were going and didn't like it), cancelled my vote for the fifth paragraph (good, but too reminiscent of the Harry Potter version of a similar folktale concept), and upvoted for everything after that.

This is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Way to make the source material even better. I love this tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you liked it. This one was really sort of a passion project-I must have wrote the entire thing in no more than a few hours. Sometimes you just need to write a certain piece, and this was such a case for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. I love the explantation for the sack, and the way you portray his feeling of remorse. It's just awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This story is quite extraordinary. You did a very good job with the whole folklore bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that was not the intention. What I meant to say is that unlike his brothers, the All Death does not appear when the death under his dominion occurs (death by forces greater than humanity- be them natural or supernatural). This includes the old man himself, but he never planned for any of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I was interpreting this passage:

It was all he had left, his final hope. He knew the only way he could force the Third to release him from his endless torment was to capture him in the sack, and thus force him and his brothers to let him die. The All Death never appeared, though, not even to mock him. When the forces of the unknown claimed a victim, only silence greeted them.

To mean that he was engineering massive devastation as bait for the Third Brother. He bears no malice towards his victims, he just needs a big enough calamity that the Third Brother can't ignore it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh no. When the old man talks about oblivion, he means his own, not that of those around him. He is willing to do more than just die-he is willing to be completely erased from existence and memory if that what it takes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this implying that he's destroying everything around him deliberately, just to lure the Third Brother so he can finally die?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done. Reminds me of the Wandering Jew, with more destruction. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's sir, and thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really fucking good. Well done, sir (or madam, I don't know for sure).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Old Man from Nowhere was a very folklore based SCP, so I thought I'd give it a proper folklore tale.
